---
title: "FAQ"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{FAQ}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

## Why is my function slow?

If a jitted `anvl` function feels sluggish, the cause is usually compilation
or startup overhead rather than the computation itself.

- **First-call compilation.** The first call with a given combination of input
  types and shapes traces the function and compiles it through XLA. This can
  take anywhere from milliseconds to seconds (or more for large functions).
  Subsequent calls with matching inputs hit the compilation cache and skip
  this step -- see [The compilation cache](jit.html#the-compilation-cache)
  in the JIT Deep Dive.
- **Recompilation on every call.** If the input shapes vary between calls,
  every new shape combination triggers a fresh recompile and you can end up
  spending more time compiling than computing. Padding inputs up to fixed
  bucket sizes lets all calls share one cache entry; see
  [Padding inputs to avoid recompilation](efficiency.html#padding-inputs-to-avoid-recompilation)
  in the Efficiency vignette.
- **Eager mode.** Running outside `jit()` compiles many tiny XLA programs --
  one per primitive call -- which adds per-call overhead and gives the
  compiler no room for whole-program optimization. See
  [Eager vs. JIT](efficiency.html#eager-vs-jit) for the trade-off.
- **Startup costs.** Loading `anvl` initializes the PJRT runtime and the
  selected backend (CPU, CUDA, ...). This is a one-time cost per R session,
  but it shows up if you benchmark a script that only runs the function once.
- **Misleading benchmarks.** Compiled calls return asynchronously, so timing
  a single call without forcing the result (e.g. via `as_array()` or
  `print()`) measures dispatch, not computation. See
  [Asynchronous execution](efficiency.html#asynchronous-execution).

The [Efficiency](efficiency.html) vignette gathers the rest of the levers
(CUDA, data types, memory donation) in one place.

## How do I control the number of threads used by XLA?

XLA (the compiler backend used by `anvl`) may use multiple CPU threads for
parallelism. On shared systems such as HPC clusters, it is often necessary
to restrict which cores a process can use.

The recommended approach is to control thread affinity **from outside the
process**, using OS-level tools. On Linux, `taskset` is the most common
option:
```bash
# Pin the R process to cores 0-3
taskset -c 0-3 Rscript my_script.R
```

See [jax#15866](https://github.com/jax-ml/jax/issues/15866) for more information.
